ocr.text: The table presents various variables related to male and female practitioners, with Male (n = 71) and Female (n = 124) as the
primary groups. The mean age in years (sd), range 24–56 for males is 32.0 (7.3), while for females it is 28.4 (3.9)†. In terms of
mean years in practice (sd), range 0.2–31.0, males average 6.2 (7.1) years compared to females, who average 3.7 (3.5)† years.
The mean number of CDE’s (sd) for males is 3.4 (4.8), whereas for females it is 2.0 (3.3)†. Regarding the place of education, %
(n), 29.6% (21) of male practitioners were educated in Nepal, while 24.2% (30) of female practitioners were educated in Nepal.
In contrast, 70.4% (50) of males pursued education abroad, and 75.8% (94) of females did the same. The sector of practice, %
(n), reveals that 27.1% (19) of males work in the public sector, while 25.2% (30) of females do; 27.1% (19) of males practice in
both public and private sectors, compared to 21.0% (25) of females, and 45.7% (32) of males are in the private sector, while
53.8% (64) of females are in the private sector. This comprehensive data illustrates the differences in demographics, education,
and practice sectors between male and female practitioners.
